question: *Elucidate the molecular mechanism by which PD-1/PD-L1 immune checkpoint blockade restores CD8+ T-cell cytolytic function in the tumor microenvironment.*
answer:
Programmed cell death protein 1 (PD-1, gene PDCD1 on chr 2q37) is an inhibitory receptor upregulated on exhausted CD8+ T cells after chronic antigen exposure. Its ligands PD-L1 (CD274, chr 9p24.1) and PD-L2 (PDCD1LG2, chr 9p24.1) are expressed on tumor cells, myeloid cells, and stromal elements. Engagement of PD-1 by PD-L1 recruits the tyrosine phosphatases SHP-1 and SHP-2 to the cytoplasmic immunoreceptor tyrosine-based inhibitory motif (ITIM), dephosphorylating proximal TCR signaling intermediates (CD3ζ, ZAP70, PKCθ) and blocking Ca2+-NFAT and Ras–MAPK pathways. Concurrently, PD-1 ligation upregulates diacylglycerol kinase-α (DGKα), diverting diacylglycerol away from RasGRP1, further suppressing ERK activation. Therapeutically, anti-PD-1 monoclonal antibodies (nivolumab, pembrolizumab) or anti-PD-L1 agents (atezolizumab, durvalumab) prevent ligand binding, restore proximal TCR signaling, normalize metabolic reprogramming via mTORC1 activation, and reinvigorate glucose uptake, mitochondrial biogenesis, and granzyme B/perforin-mediated cytotoxicity. Phase III trials (CheckMate-025, KEYNOTE-045) demonstrated objective response rates of 25–30% in clear-cell renal carcinoma and urothelial carcinoma, translating to 30–40% survival benefit at 24 months.

**Summary:** PD-1/PD-L1 blockade reverses TCR proximal inhibition via SHP-1/2 recruitment, restores metabolic fitness and cytolytic machinery in exhausted CD8+ T cells, yielding durable antitumor responses.
